lesson.menu_title: The Business Proposal — Writing a Plan That Persuades
lesson.menu_desc: Gerunds and infinitives (verb patterns) for writing a clear, persuasive proposal, built on a real business-plan reading -- 31 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 12 -- The Business Proposal
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Eliane Portela | Aula 12 -- The Business Proposal: Writing a Plan That Persuades

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: a
the short opening section that summarizes the whole proposal
b
the clear reason a customer should choose you over anyone else
c
a concrete result or item you promise to hand over
d
the logical reason or thinking behind a decision
e
an estimate of a future financial number, such as revenue
f
whether a plan can realistically be done
g
a backup plan for when something goes wrong
h
giving good value for the money it costs
i
to make a process simpler and more efficient
j
the point at which income finally equals costs
k
able to work and succeed in practice
l
a prediction of how a market or number will behave

reading.rtitle: Writing the Plan That Wins Backing
paras: Every founder who wants to raise money eventually stops reading the market and starts writing the plan. Eliane, who spent months studying her sector, has decided to put her clinic on paper. Her advisor keeps reminding her that investors dislike reading long documents, so she aims to open with a tight executive summary. It is worth spending real time on that first page: a busy investor who considers backing a clinic will often decide to keep reading, or to stop, in the first thirty seconds.

The heart of the proposal is the value proposition -- the one reason a patient chooses her clinic over the incumbent. Eliane avoids promising everything; instead, she commits to delivering three clear results, and she lists each deliverable with a rationale beside it. Her financial section is honest: the projection shows steady growth, the forecast assumes a slow first year, and the plan reaches break-even only in month eighteen. Rather than hiding the risk, she prefers to name it, and she recommends adding a contingency for the months when cash runs thin.

A good plan also proves that the idea is viable, not just exciting. Eliane's advisor suggests testing the feasibility of every promise before writing it down: can the team really deliver it, and is it cost-effective to run? She has learned to streamline the whole document -- cutting anything that does not help an investor decide. The clinic that wins backing is rarely the one with the boldest dream. It is the one whose plan is clear enough to read in ten minutes and strong enough to trust.
reading.source: Adapted from a founder's guide to writing business proposals, The Persuasive Plan

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Eliane decides that the longest, most detailed proposal always wins, so she keeps adding pages until nothing is left out.

A winning proposal is clear, honest and easy to read: it opens with a tight executive summary, states one value proposition, names its risks with a contingency, and proves the idea is viable and cost-effective.
The text argues that the financial section does not matter and that only an exciting dream persuades investors.

items: Eliane wants to open her proposal with a tight executive summary.
The text says she aims to open with a tight executive summary, because an investor decides to keep reading in the first thirty seconds.
Instead of promising everything, Eliane commits to delivering three clear results.
The text says she avoids promising everything and commits to delivering three clear results, each deliverable with a rationale.
The plan hides the risk and reaches break-even in the first month.
The text says she prefers to name the risk and adds a contingency; the plan reaches break-even only in month eighteen.
The advisor tells her to write every promise down first and test its feasibility later.
The text says the advisor suggests testing the feasibility of every promise before writing it down.

gapfill.text: Notice how the words work together: you open with an executive summary, state a value proposition, list each deliverable with its rationale, back it with a projection and forecast, plan for break-even, add a contingency, test the feasibility, keep it cost-effective, streamline the document, and prove the idea is viable. This is the language a founder uses to write a plan that persuades.

bank.label: Your proposal-writing toolkit
items: We recommend investing in one strong value proposition.
The board decided to fund the pilot for a year.
It is worth adding a contingency for a slow first year.
We aim to reach break-even in month eighteen.
Before writing the plan, test the feasibility of every promise.
Streamlining the document means cutting anything that does not help an investor decide.
They agreed to deliver three clear results, not everything at once.
A cost-effective plan avoids overspending on things patients never see.

lf.title: Gerund or infinitive? Read the verb pattern

lf.text: A, C and E use the gerund (the -ing form): after recommend, after worth, and after a preposition like before. B and D use the infinitive (to + base verb): after decide and after aim. Rule of thumb: some verbs are followed by a gerund (recommend, suggest, avoid, consider, involve), others by an infinitive (decide, aim, plan, agree, offer, manage), and any verb after a preposition takes the gerund.

items.situation: Say what you recommend for the opening of a proposal. Use 'recommend' + gerund and 'executive summary'.
tips: I recommend opening with a tight executive summary.
I recommend keeping the executive summary to one page.

items.situation: Say what your team decided about the pilot. Use 'decide' + infinitive.
tips: We decided to fund the pilot for a year.
The board decided to test the idea before scaling.

items.situation: Explain what is worth doing before you write. Use 'worth' + gerund and 'feasibility'.
tips: It is worth testing the feasibility of every promise first.
It is worth checking that the plan is cost-effective before writing it down.

items.situation: State your financial goal. Use 'aim' + infinitive and 'break-even'.
tips: We aim to reach break-even in month eighteen.
We aim to keep the first-year forecast realistic.

items: Explain how you would open your clinic's proposal. Use executive summary and value proposition, and at least two verb + gerund patterns (recommend / suggest / avoid + -ing).
Walk through your financial section: the projection, the forecast, and when you reach break-even. Use at least two verb + infinitive patterns (decide / aim / plan + to).
Describe the biggest risk and your contingency. Say what is worth doing and what you would avoid doing, using worth + gerund and avoid + gerund.
In one minute, prove your idea is viable and cost-effective. Explain how you would streamline the plan and test its feasibility, mixing gerunds and infinitives naturally.

answerkey.title: Reveal the model language
list: The summary: 'I recommend opening with a tight executive summary, and I suggest keeping the value proposition to a single sentence: the one reason a patient chooses us.'
The numbers: 'We decided to fund a slow first year. The projection shows steady growth, the forecast is honest, and we aim to reach break-even in month eighteen.'
The risk: 'It is worth adding a contingency for the tight months. I avoid promising everything, because a plan that overpromises is hard to trust.'
Prove it works: 'Before writing anything, I test the feasibility of every promise. I streamline the document, keep it cost-effective, and show that the idea is viable, not just exciting.'
answerkey.note: Notice how each point rides on a verb pattern -- recommend + -ing, decide + to, worth + -ing, aim + to. Choosing the right pattern is what makes the sentence sound native.

listenings.text: Hi Eliane, it is Marcus, your advisor. Before you send that proposal, let me give you one piece of advice: investors decide to keep reading, or to stop, on the first page. So I recommend opening with a tight executive summary, no more than one paragraph. Avoid promising everything. Choose one value proposition and defend it. In the financial section, be honest: show a realistic projection, keep the forecast modest for the first year, and say clearly when you reach break-even. It is worth adding a contingency, because a plan that hides its risk is a plan no one trusts. And before you write a single promise, test its feasibility, ask whether the team can really deliver it and whether it is cost-effective to run. Then streamline everything. Cut any page that does not help an investor decide. Keep it clear, keep it viable, and let the numbers do the persuading.

Hi team, it is Eliane. Let me recap where the proposal stands so we can finish it together. I have decided to open with a one-page executive summary, because Marcus keeps reminding me that investors stop reading fast. Our value proposition is simple: we offer one thing the incumbent does not. In the numbers, I want to avoid overpromising, so the forecast assumes a slow start and the projection reaches break-even in month eighteen. I recommend adding a contingency for the tight months, and I plan to test the feasibility of every deliverable before we write it down. Our job this week is to streamline the whole document, cutting anything that does not help a reader decide. If the plan is clear, honest and cost-effective, it proves the idea is viable. That is the plan that wins backing, and that is the one we are going to send.
